coredata.dc:description: This study presents the development of a domestic, microwave-facilitated method for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (W-CDs), utilizing waste acorn caps and thiourea as dual precursors for nitrogen and sulfur doping. The corrosion inhibiting capability of W-CDs in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid solution towards copper was assessed, showcasing remarkable inhibition efficiencies of 98.89 %, 98.71 %, and 98.42 % at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K respectively, when used at a concentration of 200 mg/L. The thermodynamic data of the adsorption process are discussed using the Arrhenius formula and the transition state equation. The adsorption of W-CDs onto copper was found to stem from a synergistic interplay of physical and chemical forces, consistent with the theoretical framework provided by the Langmuir adsorption model.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the development of a domestic, microwave-facilitated method for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (W-CDs), utilizing waste acorn caps and thiourea as dual precursors for nitrogen and sulfur doping. The corrosion inhibiting capability of W-CDs in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid solution towards copper was assessed, showcasing remarkable inhibition efficiencies of 98.89 %, 98.71 %, and 98.42 % at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K respectively, when used at a concentration of 200 mg/L. The thermodynamic data of the adsorption process are discussed using the Arrhenius formula and the transition state equation. The adsorption of W-CDs onto copper was found to stem from a synergistic interplay of physical and chemical forces, consistent with the theoretical framework provided by the Langmuir adsorption model.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the contemporary industrial landscape, copper metal stands out for its superior conductivity, thermal conductivity, and corrosion resistance, which has led to its widespread adoption across sectors such as electrical, electronics, construction, and manufacturing [1–8]. Nonetheless, the corrosion of copper under specific environmental conditions should not be overlooked, as it not only squanders resources but may also pose safety risks. The primary mode of corrosion for copper occurs electrochemically, typically in seawater, humid atmospheres, or environments containing chemical substances. To mitigate the detrimental impacts of corrosion, research and development of effective corrosion inhibitors have become a paramount priority within the domain of corrosion science, driving significant advancements in the field [9].

Corrosion inhibitors are chemicals that significantly slow down the corrosion level of metals by creating a barrier on their surface, either through chemical or electrochemical means, effectively shielding them from further attack by corrosive substances [10–21]. Traditional copper corrosion inhibitors predominantly include phosphates [22–26], sulfates [27–29], and chromates [30–33], which although effective in inhibiting corrosion, may exert adverse environmental impacts and exhibit limited efficacy in certain applications. With heightened environmental standards and an upswell in the demand for novel eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors, researchers have developed a succession of innovative inhibitors [34–36]. For instance, nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds [37] such as pyridine [38–40], imidazole [41,42], and pyrrole derivatives [43–47], which exhibit high corrosion inhibition efficiency and are environmentally benign, have garnered significant attention. Nonetheless, the long-term utilization of these inhibitors may lead to escalated costs and excessive waste of chemical reagents.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered immense interest across diverse industries and fields of application. In recent years, the scientific community's focus on CQDs has intensified, with these nanomaterials playing an increasingly pivotal role in materials science, biomedical engineering, electronics, and other domains [48–50]. The optical properties of CQDs, especially their excellent fluorescence properties, make them promising applications in biological imaging, sensors and light-emitting diodes [51–54]. CQDs exhibit good biocompatibility and do not impart toxicity or other adverse effects on cells, thereby finding extensive application in the biomedical field.

As corrosion inhibitors, CQDs also present several advantages. Their low toxicity renders them safer in applications as corrosion inhibitors. CQDs demonstrate good water solubility, enabling them to better adhere to metal surfaces and enhance corrosion inhibition efficacy. Furthermore, they can meet the various metals' needs for corrosion prevention because to their tunable electronic properties. Owing to these benefits, CQDs have piqued the interest of experts in the realm of corrosion [55–57]. For instance, the work by Xu et al. [58] who prepared CQDs using amino acid and citric acid via hydrothermal synthesis and investigated their corrosion inhibition rate and mechanism for Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4, the CQDs of 200 mg/L can reach 95.3 % corrosion suppression efficiency for Q235 steel. The study by Zhu et al. [59] who utilized folic acid and phthalic acid as precursors to fabricate N-doped CQDs, acting as a mixed inhibitor to preventing corrosion in Q235 steel with η more than 95 % in 1 M hydrochloric acid, and based on various characterization techniques, established that these N-CDs could coordinate with iron elements to form a protective film; and in Pan et al. [60] investigation CQDs derived from L-histidine and 4-amino salicylic acid, which, according to weight loss experiments and electrochemical tests, found that adding 50 mg/L of CQDs to 1 M HCl might suppress corrosion of Q235 steel by up to 93 %. Although these studies successfully produced CQDs using straightforward methods and employed them as corrosion inhibitors. However, their production often necessitates the use of toxic chemicals, which poses concerns over potential over-consumption and the resultant waste of chemical reagents. Addressing this issue, researchers have shifted their focus towards bio-derived CQDs, such as Ren et al. [61] use of peach leaves as precursors for CQDs employed as fluorescent probes in cell imaging and zebrafish experiments; Dong et al. [62] obtained carbon quantum dots by solid phase pyrolysis, and used it for the high corrosion inhibitors agent of Q235 steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid. Employing both weight loss experiments and electrochemical testing, it was determined that the corrosion mitigation performance for Q235 steel achieved an impressive 95 % efficiency. Long et al. [63] the biomass carbon point CD was synthesized by green hydrothermal method. They used electrochemical impedance to test the CD, and the corrosion relief performance of Q235 steel prepared from litchi leaf reached 98 % in 1 M HCL. Based on this, with biomass as precursor system for carbon quantum dot is a kind of environmental protection and successful ideas, but the predecessors of biomass carbon quantum dot corrosion inhibitor mostly pay attention to explore Q235 steel in salt solution or hydrochloric acid solution and some other metals corrosion performance, and the research of carbon quantum dots as copper inhibitor in 0.5 M H2SO4 is generally less. In addition, for the preparation method of CQDs, in addition to the simple and convenient synthesis method of hydrothermal synthesis, it is known that the preparation time for CQDs may be significantly reduced by using microwave synthesis, and that the particles will also be smaller.

In this study, we synthesized carbon quantum dot corrosion inhibitors (W-CDs) utilizing a common household microwave oven as a reaction vessel and discarded acorn caps as precursor materials. Acorns, one of the most common nuts, are abundant and widely spread, making them easy to collect and consume. Acorn caps, on the other hand, are either dumped as garbage or turned into processed into ornaments. There is no technical answer in the known articles for generating carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors from acorn caps. We examined the produced W-CDs by conducting electrochemical tests, morphological analyses, and investigating the adsorption mechanism. Thus, a new biomass carbon quantum dots corrosion inhibitor and its anti-corrosion capabilities were discovered.

First of all, the dried acorn cap is crushed in a pulverizer, and the crushed powder is passed through a 500 mesh sieve for later use. Then, 3.0 g of this acorn cap powder and an equal amount of thiourea (3.0 g) are added to a 100-mL beaker. Subsequently, approximately 20 mL of pure water is poured into the beaker as the solvent, and the mixture is subjected to sonication for a duration of 20 min. After 2–4 min reaction in an 800 W household microwave oven, the brown solid was attached to the bottom of the beaker. After 20 ml of pure water was sonicated for 30 min, most of the unreacted large particles passed through the funnel, and the remaining impurities were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous filter. After that, unreacted tiny molecules were dialyzed into a dialysis bag at a concentration of roughly 1000 Da for a full day. Every 8 h throughout this time, the ultrapure water was changed. At the completion of dialysis, the liquid in the bag is moved to the refrigerator, frozen for 24 h, and then dried in a freeze dryer until powdered carbon quantum dots are ready to use The preparation process of carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 1 .

Electrochemical evaluations were performed adopting the standard three-electrode configuration, with a 99.9 % pure copper serving as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode as the reference, and platinum foil functioning as the counter electrode. Before each electrochemical test, the working electrode underwent abrasive polishing using progressively finer grades of sandpaper, starting from 400 and culminating at 2000 grit, for thorough water-based grinding.

Within the electrochemical assessments, the initial phase involved the determination of the open-circuit potential (E
ocp ), which lasted for 40 min. Following this, EIS was conducted across a broad frequency spectrum, from 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz, with an alternating current (AC) voltage amplitude of 0.01 V. The Tafel experiment was performed within ±0.25 V of the E
ocp , at a scan rate of 0.001 V/s. To minimize experimental error, the experiments were executed within a controlled water bath, and each measurement was replicated three times.

A solution of dilute sulfuric acid, prepared from 98 % concentrated sulfuric acid to a concentration of 0.5 M, served as the blank solution, with W-CDs of varying concentrations added as the test solution.

Copper samples having a purity of 99.9 % are utilized to conduct corrosion surface analysis. There are two types: one with a volume of 1 × 1 × 1 cm3 for AFM and XPS, and another with a volume of 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 cm3 for SEM. Prior to each use, the copper surface is water-sanded with 400–7000 grit sandpaper until it is brilliant and smooth. The pretreated samples were submerged in the experimental and blank solutions in a water bath with constant temperatures set to 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K for 24 h.

TEM can be used to observe the micromorphology and size of nanoparticles. W-CDs were observed on ultra-thin carbon film, as seen in the Fig. 2 , W-CDs are evenly distributed, with obvious spherical structure, and all sizes are below 10 nm. TEM results proved the synthesis of W-CDs.

The functional group information of W-CDs was examined, and their absorption spectrum properties were analyzed, using FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in the Fig. 3 , the peak at approximately 3380 cm−1 in the W-CDs spectrum is attributed to -OH groups [64], while the stretching vibrations of primary or secondary amines are responsible for the peak at 3270 cm−1, hydrogen bond in W-CDs is responsible for the widening of both peaks [65]. The double bond interactions between N=C=S and N=C=O bonds are responsible for the distinctive absorption peak at 2070 cm−1 [66–68]. The typical absorption of CC and CN bonds is represented by the absorption peak at 1610 cm−1 [69,70]. CN bonds are responsible for the absorption peak at 1460 cm−1 [71–73], while the peak at 1420 cm−1 is due to -CH3. The possible origin of the absorption peak at 1080 cm−1 is C-SO3 [74]. Below 1000 cm−1, the characteristic absorption peaks are attributed to the bending vibrations of aromatic or aliphatic CH bonds.

Fig. 4 depicts the variations in both the OCP and the Tafel curves of Cu when immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, with these alterations observed across various concentrations of the solution. The following are the copper reactions in H2SO4, as recorded in the literature [75,76]:

To further explore the corrosion suppression properties of W-CDs, we fitted the polarization curves by extrapolation, with the corresponding data as seen in Table 1 . R
corr and η can be obtained by formula (4)and formula (5) [60,81–88,89-94]:

Table 1 reveals a clear trend: as the concentration increases, the i
corr diminishes, the E
corr shifts towards more negative values, and consequently, the ƞ enhances. As the temperature rises, the i
corr rises, the E
corr shifts to the positive, and the ƞ declines. At 298 K, by adding 200 mg/L W-CDs, the ƞ is increased to 91.29 %. Additionally, W-CDs results in a significant shift of the E
corr by over 85 mV at varying temperature, in comparison to the untreated solution. This substantial change underscores the efficacy of W-CDs as cathodic corrosion inhibitors [12,95–96].

Different amounts of W-CDs were present in the sulfuric acid solutions that the copper electrodes were submerged in, and the Nyquist plots at various temperatures are depicted in Fig. 5 (a, b, c). The spectra reveal that in the high-frequency region, a flattened semicircle is discernible, which is indicative of both the double-layer capacitance and the charge transfer resistance [97–99]. The low-frequency region displays a small tail (is attributed to Warburg impedance, which primarily arises from the solution's dissolved oxygen and the diffusion of the inhibitor material) [100,101]. Remarkably, the incorporation of the inhibitor leads to the elimination of the Warburg impedance, signifying a hindered diffusion of dissolved oxygen within the solution. Additionally, the semicircle resistance diameter of the Nyquist curve increases with the concentration of W-CDs, yet the shape remains same from that of the blank solution, suggesting that W-CDs have the ability to coat the copper electrode's surface in a protective layer, efficiently preventing copper degradation in sulfuric acid solution while maintaining the electrode's intrinsic electrochemical behavior.

Fig. 5(d, e, f) presents the Bode plots at different temperatures, revealing significantly broader and higher impedance magnitude and phase angle, indicative of the superior corrosion suppression performance of W-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5, as the number of carbon quantum dots increases, all impedance mode values increase and the phase angle increases. The larger the phase angle, the more carbon quantum dots are adsorbed on the copper electrode. Critically, the phase angle plot reveals two distinct maxima, indicating the presence of two distinct time constants, which arise from two distinct relaxation processes linked to the adsorption behavior of W-CDs onto the electrode. The first is ascribed to the double-layer capacitance, whereas the second corresponds to the binding and release of W-CDs at the electrode interface [102,103]. The figure illustrates a notable intensification of the two peaks in the phase angle as the temperature rises, providing evidence for the enhanced adsorption and desorption dynamics of W-CDs occurring on the electrode surface.

The obtained impedance data was modeled using the equivalent circuit schematic in Fig. 6 . Where the warburg impedance, solution resistance, membrane resistance, and charge transfer resistance are, in order, represented by the symbols ω, R

The essential parameters are listed in Table 2 . The R
ct and R
p are much greater than the blank solution, while the CPE
dl and CPE
f are essentially smaller. The inhibitor molecules are found to form an adsorption layer onto the metal interface, effectively hindering the diffusion of copper ions within the corrosive medium. At 298 K, when 200 mg/L of W-CDs was applied, the η reached 98.89 %. Concurrently, it was observed that an increase in temperature led to a decrease in the R
ct , suggesting that the corrosion rate of Cu is influenced by temperature and accelerates with higher temperatures. Notably, at 318 K, the corrosion efficiency (η) remained high, approximately 98 %, indicating that W-CDs serve as an effective corrosion inhibitor, continuing to offer robust inhibition within a defined temperature range.

The morphological alterations of copper surfaces following immersion in in blank solution or experiment solution under various temperature 24 h are depicted in Fig. 7 . At 298 K, the Cu surface in Fig. 7a exhibits corrosion-induced traces along with distinct pitting corrosion features. Conversely, at the same temperature as depicted in Fig. 7d, the test sample soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 with W-CDs presents distinct scratch marks without evidence of significant corrosion. At 308 K, Fig. 7b shows copper surfaces with dense corrosion traces, and additional cracking is observed. The copper surface in Fig. 7e appears comparatively flat with visible scratch marks, suggesting that the copper is not excessively corroded. In contrast, the copper surface in Fig. 7c, with heavily congested traces, indicates severe corrosion. The copper surface in Fig. 7f exhibits some corrosion traces, yet the surface retains a relatively flat configuration. These findings corroborate that the incorporation of W-CDs into the 0.5 M H2SO4 can effectively mitigate copper corrosion, and the propensity for copper corrosion intensifies with rising temperature.

Fig. 8 shows the 3D morphologies of material surfaces after immersion for 24 h in blank solution or experiment solution at various temperatures. Fig. 9 shows the relative height contrast maps of copper surfaces at the same temperature. As depicted in Fig. 8(a, b, c), the copper surface soak in blank solution exhibits an uneven texture with pronounced corrosion-induced traces. With increasing temperature, the corrosion traces become larger, and the surface roughness increases from 48.54 to 130.84. In contrast, when copper is immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing W-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 8(d, e, f), the surfaces are relatively flat, and the traces of sandpaper abrasion still persist, indicating that the surface is not severely corroded. Concurrently, with the rise in temperature, the surface roughness increases from 10 to approximately 30. The AFM results indicate that W-CDs exhibit significant inhibitory effects on corrosion, which however, weaken with increasing temperature, corroborating the electrochemical testing and scanning results.。.

XPS spectroscopy can help us understand the content and bonding of W-CDs molecules on material surfaces. The XPS spectrum of W-CDs on the Cu surface is depicted in Fig. 10 . In Fig. 10 b, the peak associated with Cu 2p appeared. Cu (I) 2p 3/2 and Cu (I) 2p1/2 are the values that correspond to the peaks at 932.73 eV and 952.93 eV, respectively [113]. Fig. 10c represents the correlation peak of C 1 s, with three peaks detected at 284.38 eV, 286.38 eV and 288.13 eV from C-C/C-N, C-O-C and O=C-O [114], respectively. Fig. 10d shows the characteristic peaks of the O 1 s, the peaks of CuO/Cu2O as well as C-O-C can be found at 531.88 eV and 533.48 eV, indicating that W-CDs adsorbed on the Cu surface [115]. Fig. 10e presents the XPS peak spectrum of S 2p after adsorption of W-CDs on the copper surface. There are three peaks in the spectrum, among which the presence of SCu bond at 163.38 eV indicates that W-CDs form a chemical bond with the copper surface [116]. Fig. 10f shows the spectrum of N 1 s, the NCu bond at 400.08 eV further confirms that W-CDs can adsorb on the material surface and establish chemical bonds with it.

To probe the adsorption mechanism of W-CDs at the copper/electrolyte interface more thoroughly, we used a number of adsorption models to analyze the electrochemical impedance spectrum, among which only the R2 of the linear regression equation of the Langmuir adsorption model was infinitely close to 1, so we finally used the Langmuir adsorption model for analysis. Eq. (10) provides an expression for the Langmuir adsorption model [117–120]:
(10)

The usual free energy of adsorption (ΔGads 0) can be calculated by the following formula(11) [97,121–123]:
(11)

A larger ΔGads 0 indicates a greater adsorption effect of W-CDs on the material's surface. Moreover, the positivity or negativity of ΔGads 0 determines whether the adsorption of W-CDs is spontaneous (if ΔGads 0 is less than 0, the adsorption is spontaneous; otherwise, it is not). As depicted in Fig. 11 , the ΔGads 0 values at different temperatures are all negative, indicating that W-CDs can adsorb on the copper surface spontaneously at various temperatures. Crucially, the kind of adsorption of W-CDs may be determined by the ΔGads 0 value. The adsorption of W-CDs on the metal surface is chemical in nature if the ΔGads 0 value is less than −40 kJ/mol. When the ΔGads 0 value exceeds −20 kJ/mol, it suggests that there is electrostatic attraction between the charged W-CDs and the charged metal, indicating physical adsorption [124,125]. Fig. 12 shows other isothermal adsorption models such as Temkin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic study of the adsorption process
ce:section.ce:para:
To learn more about the kinetics of N, S-CDs adsorption on the copper, we apply the Arrhenius formula and the formulae for transition states, which are given by formula (12) and (13), respectively:
(12)
lnR corr
= lnA −
E a
RT
(13)
R corr
=
RT Nh
exp
∆
S a
R
exp
− ∆
H a
RT

In which, A is the prefinger factor, N is Avogadro's constant, h is Planck's constant and R
corr is the corrosion rate.

Fig. 13 show the fitted plots for the Arrhenius and transition state equations and Table 3 displays the adsorption kinetic parameters (enthalpy of activation ∆H
a , activation energy E
a , and entropy of activation ∆S
a ) derived by fits and calculations. The results show that copper has a lower activation energy in the sulfuric acid blank solution. In the absence of an oxide film, copper is easily corroded, resulting in Cu2+. After the addition of W-CDs, the W-CDs are coordinated with copper to form a protective film, which slows down the corrosion rate of copper, but this process requires energy absorption, so the activation energy of copper after the addition of W-CDs increases. The positive or negative enthalpy of activation represents whether the reaction process needs to absorb energy or release energy, the activation enthalpy of copper in sulfuric acid solution is positive, which means that the dissolution of copper needs to absorb energy, and the activation enthalpy after the addition of W-CDs increases, indicating that the dissolution of copper becomes more difficult. In addition, the decrease of ∆Sa after the addition of W-CDs represents a decrease in the disorder of the system, indicating that W-CDs enhance the order of the reaction, possibly because the W-CDs molecules create a persistent adsorption protective film on the surface of the material.

The concurrent integration of theoretical and experimental findings has elucidated the corrosion inhibition mechanism as illustrated in Fig. 14 . On one hand, physical adsorption occurs between W-CDs and the metal, where the W-CDs are electrostatically adsorbed onto the copper surface. Alternatively stated, the incorporation of heteroatoms such as N and S in W-CDs, which possess electron-donating capabilities, coupled with the copper surface's electron-accepting vacant orbitals, facilitates the establishment of robust coordination bonds between W-CDs and Cu. This, in turn, triggers the formation of a barrier on the Cu surface, effectively shielding it from direct exposure to corrosive media and thereby exerting a corrosion inhibitory effect.

ce:list-item.ce:para: TEM confirms the successful synthesis of W-CDs, with sizes of the synthesized W-CDs being below 10 nm.
EIS data indicate that W-CDs exhibit superior corrosion inhibition properties in 0.5 M H2SO4. At a concentration of 200 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency reaches 98.89 %, 98.71 %, 98.42 %.
XPS analysis reveals the formation of new bonds, such as SCu and NCu, which confirms the presence of chemical interactions between W-CDs and copper.
The binding of W-CDs to copper interfaces is governed by a combination of physical and chemical mechanisms, as suggested by the analysis according to the Langmuir adsorption model.

ce:section.ce:para: Bochuan Tan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yan Liu: Software, Formal analysis. Wenting Zhao: Software, Project administration. Zhili Gong: Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Xin Li: Supervision, Resources. Wenpo Li: Resources, Methodology. Xianghong Li: Validation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Lei Guo: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Wenyan Zhang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Shijie Zhao: Investigation. Riadh Marzouki: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Qingwei Dai: Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xi Chen: Software, Methodology.
